title: St. Silvanus, St. Mary of Egypt, St. Melitene & St. Gerontius of Cervia
narrative:
title": "St. Silvanus, St. Mary of Egypt, St. Melitene & St. Gerontius of Cervia

narrative": "title\": \"St. Silvanus, St. Mary of Egypt, St. Melitene & St. Gerontius of Cervia

narrative\": \"St. Silvanus the Bishop-Martyr, St. Mary of Egypt, St. Melitene of Marcianopolis, and St. Gerontius of Cervia

On the first day of Miazia, the holy Church opens the month of dawn with four witnesses who together stand at the threshold between the shadow of the fast and the first light of the approaching Resurrection. Saint Silvanus the Bishop-Martyr is a confessor of the Palestinian church who was arrested during the Diocletianic persecution and condemned to the mines of Phaeno in the Negev desert — those terrible copper mines to which the empire sent its most dangerous criminals and its most obstinate religious dissidents, working them in chains until their bodies gave out. Silvanus was bishop of Gaza, and the tradition records that he was executed alongside thirty-nine companions after years of labour in the mines, so that his martyrdom was doubly a witness to the truth: first through the long suffering of the captivity, then through the final gift of blood. In Miazia's dawning light, he represents those who endure the long night of the fast to receive the morning's crown.

Saint Mary of Egypt returns at the opening of Miazia because her story, already told at the close of Megabit, is precisely the story of a Lenten passage reaching its completion. The month of Miazia is the month of her commemoration in the full sense: it is in Miazia that Zosimas carried the Eucharist to her across the Jordan, and it is here that the tradition places the final act of her extraordinary penitential life. The woman who could not enter the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem now receives the Body of Christ in the open desert, carried by a wandering priest in a wooden vessel, with the Jordan River as her nave and the stars as her canopy. The dawn of Miazia is Mary's dawn — the dawn of one who has come through forty-seven years of darkness into the light of the resurrection she has been approaching all along.

Saint Melitene of Marcianopolis is this day's discovery from the eastern Balkans: a young Christian woman of that Thracian city who was arrested during the Decian persecution and who, the tradition records, bore her torments with a serenity so complete that the soldiers assigned to execute her came away from the day unsettled and changed. Melitene's name means honey in Greek, and the tradition's preservation of it is a small act of poetry: that the one whose name means sweetness should have been present at the bitterest of human experiences and transformed them. She is the dawn-witness of this day, the sweetness that awaits at the end of the night.

Saint Gerontius of Cervia is among the most unexpected figures in the Miazia assembly: a hermit of the Adriatic coast near the ancient city of Caesarea Maritima whose life of solitary prayer gave him, according to the tradition, a gift of healing that drew the sick from the surrounding countryside to his cell. He lived at the edge of the sea, and the tradition's preservation of this geographical detail is not incidental: the shore is the meeting place of two worlds, the boundary where the solid earth of ordinary life meets the immensity of the ocean, and the hermit who inhabits that boundary is living out in geography the interior condition of the person who stands between the world of time and the world of eternity. On the first day of Miazia, Gerontius's liminal dwelling is the image of the Church's own position — on the shore between Lent and Easter, between the fast and the feast, watching the dawn light gather on the water. Amen.
